  • LoyaltyService: The new remote microservice (Project B) that calculates discounts.

Before executing any new logic, we ask the Feature Flag service if the loyalty-discount feature is active for the current user.

  • We send a GET request to the /evaluate endpoint, passing the userId as the identifier. This allows for Direct Delivery (e.g., enabling the feature only for specific users).
  • Notice the try/catch block around the flag check. If the Feature Flag service is down or unreachable, we catch the error and default isFeatureEnabled to false. This ensures the Shop Service never crashes just because the flag service is having a hiccup.

This is the core of the "Latent Code" delivery technique. The code inside the if (isFeatureEnabled) block is deployed to production but remains dormant until the flag is switched on.

  • If the flag is ON, we attempt to make a real HTTP call to the Loyalty Service.
  • We send the userId to Service B and wait for a calculated discount percentage.
  • If the Loyalty Service is down (or not even deployed yet), the catch block handles the error gracefully. The user simply gets the standard price, and the error is logged. This allows us to deploy Service A before Service B is ready.

Finally, the service returns the response.

  • If the flag was OFF, the user sees the originalAmount and a standard message.
  • If the flag was ON (and succeeded), the user sees the finalPrice and a loyalty message.

Now, with the flag off, we can see the following answer from our Shop Service.

{
  "@odata.context": "$metadata#ShopService.CheckoutResponse",
  "userId": "1",
  "originalAmount": 888,
  "discountPercentage": 0,
  "finalPrice": 888,
  "message": "Standard price applied."
}

If we enable our flag, it is expected that the process will fail, because service B is not developed yet. It is important that you enable the flags at the proper moment.

{
  "@odata.context": "$metadata#ShopService.CheckoutResponse",
  "userId": "1",
  "originalAmount": 220,
  "discountPercentage": 0,
  "finalPrice": 220,
  "message": "Standard price applied (Loyalty Service unavailable)."
}

Now, let’s develop Service B. In a separate project, we define the services and the logic.

service LoyaltyService {

    type DiscountResponse {
        userId : String;
        percentage : Integer;
        tier : String;
    }

    // The endpoint Service A will call
    function getDiscount(userId: String) returns DiscountResponse;
}
const cds = require('@sap/cds');

module.exports = async function () {

    this.on('getDiscount', async (req) => {
        const { userId } = req.data;

        // Default State
        let percentage = 0;
        let tier = 'Bronze';

        // Simulation Logic
        if (userId) {
            const id = userId.toLowerCase();

            if (id.startsWith('vip')) {
                percentage = 20;
                tier = 'Platinum';
            } else if (id.startsWith('a')) {
                percentage = 10;
                tier = 'Gold';
            }
        }

        console.log(`[Loyalty Service] Calculated discount for ${userId}: ${percentage}% (${tier})`);

        return {
            userId: userId,
            percentage: percentage,
            tier: tier
        };
    });
}

Now, after deploying our application, and knowing that we are confident enough to provide this new functionality to our end users, then we enable the feature flag.

After enabling it, and executing our checkout process, we can see that the new functionality is released.

{
  "@odata.context": "$metadata#ShopService.CheckoutResponse",
  "userId": "vip",
  "originalAmount": 889,
  "discountPercentage": 20,
  "finalPrice": 711.2,
  "message": "Loyalty Applied! Service B gave you 20% off."
}

Instantly—without restarting the Shop Service—the application behavior changes.

  1. Shop Service detects the flag is active.
  2. It opens the communication channel to Loyalty Service.
  3. Loyalty Service receives the request, identifies the user as a VIP, and returns a 20% discount.
  4. Shop Service calculates the final price and returns the "Loyalty Applied" message.

What we have just demonstrated is Synchronized Delivery.

In a real-world SAP BTP landscape, you might have the "Shop Team" and the "Loyalty Team" working on different sprints.

  1. The Shop Team deployed their code (with the flag OFF) on Monday.
  2. The Loyalty Team deployed their service on Wednesday.
  3. On Friday, the Product Owner clicked "Enable" in the dashboard.

Both services started working together instantly, with zero downtime and zero code rollbacks. This is the power of decoupling deployment from release.

Enhancing our rollout experience:

We have successfully orchestrated our services, but right now, the feature is either ON for everyone or OFF for everyone.

In a real-world scenario, you rarely roll out a major new feature to 100% of your users immediately. You want to test it with a trusted group first—perhaps your internal team or a set of "Beta" customers. This is where Direct Delivery comes in.

We don't need to change a single line of code to achieve this. Our code is already sending the identifier (userId) to the service.

So, let’s open our feature flag in our feature flag instance and let’s define a Direct Delivery Strategy, in which we define that only users vip01, and a01, will be able to experiment this new feature.

Now, if we try to apply a discount for vip02, it will not even reach/call service B, because the toggle is OFF for that user. Even if the Service B contemplates all kind of users that start with “vip”.

{
  "@odata.context": "$metadata#ShopService.CheckoutResponse",
  "userId": "vip02",
  "originalAmount": 498,
  "discountPercentage": 0,
  "finalPrice": 498,
  "message": "Standard price applied."
}
{
  "@odata.context": "$metadata#ShopService.CheckoutResponse",
  "userId": "vip01",
  "originalAmount": 445,
  "discountPercentage": 20,
  "finalPrice": 356,
  "message": "Loyalty Applied! Service B gave you 20% off."
}

You can also perform a percentage delivery. In my case, I have defined a 50/50 percentage, so the half of my users will take advantage of my new feature, meanwhile others will not be able to use it.

For example, vip06 is not being able to use this new feature, but vip33, can access to it.

{
  "@odata.context": "$metadata#ShopService.CheckoutResponse",
  "userId": "vip06",
  "originalAmount": 725,
  "discountPercentage": 0,
  "finalPrice": 725,
  "message": "Standard price applied."
}
{
  "@odata.context": "$metadata#ShopService.CheckoutResponse",
  "userId": "vip33",
  "originalAmount": 635,
  "discountPercentage": 20,
  "finalPrice": 508,
  "message": "Loyalty Applied! Service B gave you 20% off."
}

---

Personal Thoughts:

Let me share some personal thoughts from using the SAP Feature Flag Service and while redacting this blog post.

Regarding the setup and initial steps to get this service up and running. Consider that it is already available under the Build Code plan. This accelerates the process of incorporating it into your projects. The documentation is very accurate, and with the quick start guide, you can begin using the service in minutes. The only advice I can give, if you really want to take advantage of this service, is to take the time to understand its capabilities and how to adapt them to your needs. This is key to be fully aware of the benefits. The documentation is well-structured, is fast to read, and clearly explains many of the topics involved in using the service. You will find straightforward explanations about the different release methods, what you can achieve, how to perform the setup and how to maintain the feature flags.

Regarding the usage. I really think this is a very powerful tool. It helps developers and business experts agree on the release, coordinating efforts so everyone knows exactly when a feature is ready to go live. One of the most important aspects of releasing new features is the confidence of the project members prior to the launch of a new enhancement. In distributed systems, many factors must be considered during deployment. Even with unit tests, integration tests, and manual tests in place, there are always some concerns when releasing enhancements. Having flags that can enable and disable these features reduces those concerns significantly. By configuring the proper feature flags, you can test with end users before the official release. This ensures you get feedback on time and can improve the tool before everyone else sees it, increasing the confidence prior launch (and reducing possible issues in production).

Another important aspect that all project members should consider is the "next steps" after using these flags. Be careful, having too many blocks inside conditional statements can lead to complicated code (and nesting feature flags can also lead to complicated debugging sessions). Hence, the decommissioning of feature flags is an important step in the enhancement lifecycle. In my opinion, removing the flags in the code becomes a mandatory task if you want to keep your project clean and maintainable. So, do not underestimate the cleanup steps after releasing a new enhancement with this service.

---

Wrap Up:

We have journeyed from a simple, standalone "Shop Service" to a sophisticated, distributed system that orchestrates pricing logic with a "Loyalty Service."

By introducing the SAP Feature Flag Service, we didn't just add a toggle; we fundamentally changed how we deliver software.

  • Decoupling: We deployed Service A's orchestration logic before Service B was even ready.
  • Safety: We ensured that if Service B fails, Service A degrades gracefully to standard pricing.
  • Targeting: With Direct Delivery and Perncetage Delivery, we rolled out the new feature exclusively to a set of users, testing in production with zero risk to the end users.

This is the essence of modern cloud-native development on SAP Build Code. It is not just about writing code; it is about controlling when and how that code provides value to your users.

The SAP Feature Flag Service has even more depth, including complex rule sets and other capabilities that can be check in the following guide. If you find this topic interesting, let me know in the comments, and we can dedicate a future "Deep Dive" post to check more capabilities.

I hope you find this post useful.

Kind regards,

Nata.

entry.title: SAP Build Code – Technical Series – SAP Continuous Integration and Delivery
entry.content:
Guide to CI/CD in SAP Build Code

Introduction

Continuous Integration and Continuous Delivery (CI/CD) is a crucial practice in modern software development, enabling teams to deliver high-quality code faster and more reliably. SAP Build Code provides a robust CI/CD service that integrates seamlessly with your development workflow, automating the build, test, and deployment processes.

In this guide, we'll walk through setting up and configuring a complete CI/CD pipeline for a SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) application. We'll cover everything from basic setup to advanced configurations, with real examples and screenshots at every step.

What You'll Learn

  • Setting up your first CI/CD pipeline in SAP Build Code - CI/CD Service
  • Advanced job configuration with repository-based config
  • Automating builds with webhooks and triggers
  • Implementing multi-branch strategies for feature development
  • Integrating SAP CI/CD build status with GitHub Pull Requests

Prerequisites

Before we begin, ensure you have:

  • SAP BTP account with access to SAP Continuous Integration & Delivery service
  • Git repository (In this blog GitHub is going to be used)
  • Basic knowledge of CAP (Cloud Application Programming Model)
  • Node.js and npm installed locally
  • @sap/cds-dk installed

Our Sample Application

For this guide, we'll use a simple CAP application with a "Hello World" service endpoint. While minimal, this application demonstrates all the key CI/CD concepts without unnecessary complexity.

Repository structure:

cicd-service/
├── .sap_cid/
│   └── config.yaml              # CI/CD pipeline configuration
├── srv/
│   ├── hello-service.cds       # Service definition
│   └── hello-service.js        # Service implementation
├── tests/
│   └── hello-service.test.js   # Unit tests
├── jest.config.js              # Test configuration
├── mta.yaml                    # Deployment descriptor
├── package.json                # Dependencies
└── xs-security.json            # Security config

---

Part 1: Setting Up Your First CI/CD Pipeline

Step 1.1: Create Your CAP Application

First, let's look at our simple CAP application structure.

Service Definition (srv/hello-service.cds):

service HelloService {
   function sayHello() returns String;
}

Service Implementation (srv/hello-service.js):

module.exports = (srv) => {
   srv.on('sayHello', () => { return 'Hello World from SAP Build Code CI/CD!'; 
   });
};

This minimal service provides a single function that returns a greeting message.

Step 1.2: Add Unit Tests

Quality assurance starts with testing. We'll use Jest to test our service.

Test File (tests/hello-service.test.js):

const cds = require('@sap/cds/lib');
const path = require('path');

describe('Hello Service', () => {
    let srv;

    beforeAll(async () => {
        // Load the CDS model
        const csn = await cds.load(path.join(__dirname, '../srv/hello-service.cds'));

        // Deploy the model to an in-memory database
        await cds.deploy(csn);

        // Get the service
        srv = await cds.serve('HelloService').from(csn);
    });

    test('sayHello returns correct message', async () => {
        const result = await srv.tx().send('sayHello');
        expect(result).toBe('Hello World from SAP Build Code CI/CD!');
    });
});

Test locally:

npm install
npm test

You should see:

PASS  tests/hello-service.test.js
  Hello Service
    ✓ sayHello returns correct message (4 ms)

------------------|---------|----------|---------|---------|-------------------
File              | % Stmts | % Branch | % Funcs | % Lines | Uncovered Line #s
------------------|---------|----------|---------|---------|-------------------
All files         |     100 |      100 |     100 |     100 |
 hello-service.js |     100 |      100 |     100 |     100 |
------------------|---------|----------|---------|---------|-------------------

Step 1.3: Initialize Git Repository

# Initialize Git
git init

# Add all files
git add .

# Create initial commit
git commit -m "Initial commit: Simple CAP application for CI/CD demonstration"

Step 1.4: Push to GitHub

Create a new repository on GitHub (without initializing it), then:

# Add remote (replace YOUR-USERNAME with your GitHub username)
git remote add origin https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/YOUR-REPO.git

# Push to GitHub
git push -u origin main

Screenshot 1.1: GitHub repository overview showing all files (.sap_cid will appear later on the exercise).

Step 1.5: Access SAP Continuous Integration & Delivery

  1. Open your SAP BTP Cockpit
  2. Navigate to your subaccount
  3. Go to Services → Instances and Subscriptions
  4. Find Continuous Integration & Delivery
  5. Click to open the service

Screenshot 1.2: SAP CI/CD service in BTP Cockpit

Step 1.6: Add Your Repository

In the SAP CI/CD service:

  1. Click Repositories in the top menu
  2. Click + (Add) button
  3. Configure your repository:
  • Name: cicd-service
  • Clone URL: Your GitHub repository URL
  • Credentials: Add your GitHub credentials (I have used a Personal Access Token in this case)

Screenshot 1.3: Adding repository to SAP CI/CD

Step 1.7: Create Your First Job

Now let's create a CI/CD job using the Job Editor (GUI-based configuration):

  1. Click Jobs in the top bar.
  2. Click + (Create Job)
  3. Configure the job:
  • Job Name: cicd-service-main
  • Repository: Select cicd-service
  • Branch: main
  • Pipeline: Select Cloud Foundry Environment

Screenshot 1.5: Creating a new job in Job Editor

Step 1.8: Configure Build Stage

In the Stages tab, configure the Build stage:

  • Build Tool: mta
  • Build Tool Version: Java 21 Node 22
  • Build Descriptor: mta.yaml

Screenshot 1.6: Build stage configuration

Step 1.9: Configure Additional Unit Tests Stage

Enable and configure the Additional Unit Tests stage:

  • npm Script: test
  • Build Descriptor: package.json

Screenshot 1.7: Test stage configuration

Step 1.10: Save and Run Your First Build

  1. Click Create to save the job
  2. Click Run to trigger your first build
  3. Watch the pipeline execute!

Screenshot 1.9: Build successful with all stages passed

Click on any stage to view detailed logs.

 Congratulations! You've successfully set up your first CI/CD pipeline in SAP Build Code!

---

Part 2: Advanced Job Configuration with Source Repository Config

So far, we've used the Job Editor (GUI) to configure our pipeline. While user-friendly, it has limitations for complex configurations. Let's explore Source Repository Configuration mode, which uses a configuration file in your repository for more flexibility and version control.

2.1: Why Use Source Repository Configuration?

Benefits:

  • Configuration as code - version controlled with your application
  • More advanced options and customization
  • Easier to replicate across projects
  • Supports custom commands and scripts
  • Better for team collaboration
  • Changes tracked in Git history

2.2: Export Your Job Configuration to YAML

SAP CI/CD makes it easy to transition from Job Editor to repository configuration by allowing you to export your current settings. This is a great practice as it gives you a template to start with...

Steps to export configuration:

  1. In SAP Continuous Integration and Delivery, navigate to your job `cicd-service-main`
  2. Go to the Stages tab
  3. Click YML (next to the Job Editor button)
  4. Click Copy to copy the YAML configuration to your clipboard

Screenshot 2.1: Exporting job configuration to YAML

Screenshot 2.2: YAML configuration dialog

2.3: Understanding the Configuration Structure

Build Stage:

stages:
  build:
    buildTool: "mta"                    # Build tool (mta, npm, maven, etc.)
    buildDescriptor: "mta.yaml"         # MTA descriptor file
    buildToolVersion: "MBTJ21N22"       # MBT with Java 21 and Node 22

Malware Scan Stage:

  malwareScan:
    scan: false                 # Enable/disable security scanning

Additional Tests Stage:

  additionalTests:
    npmTests:
      buildDescriptor: "package.json"
      npmScript: "test"        # npm script to run

2.4: Create Configuration File in Repository

Now let's add this configuration to our repository. SAP CI/CD looks for configuration in `.sap_cid/config.yaml`:

Create the configuration directory and file:

# Create directory
mkdir .sap_cid

# Create config file
touch .sap_cid/config.yaml

Add the prior exported configuration to `.sap_cid/config.yaml`.

Screenshot 2.4: config.yaml file in repository

2.5: Switch Job to Source Repository Configuration

Now let's switch our job to use this repository configuration:

  1. In SAP CI/CD, navigate to your job `cicd-service-main`
  2. Click Edit
  3. In the Stages tab, find the Configuration Mode dropdown
  4. Change from Job Editor to Source Repository
  5. Click Save

Screenshot 2.5: Switching to Source Repository configuration mode

Important: Once you switch to Source Repository mode, the Job Editor will not have the configuration steps. All configuration changes must be made in the `.sap_cid/config.yaml` file and committed to Git.

2.6: Commit and Push Configuration

Let's commit our new configuration to Git:

git add .sap_cid/config.yaml
git commit -m "Add Source Repository configuration for CI/CD pipeline"
git push

2.7: Verify Configuration is Applied

Trigger a new build to verify the configuration is being read from the repository:

Screenshot 2.8: Build using Source Repository configuration

2.8: Add Custom Commands to Build Stage

Now let's customize our pipeline by adding a custom command that runs during the build stage. We'll create an npm script and execute it using the `runFirst` command. This demonstrates how to add custom logic while using the MTA build tool.

First, add a custom script to `package.json`:

{
  "name": "cicd-service",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "scripts": {
    "start": "cds-serve",
    "test": "jest",
    "build": "cds build --production",
    "build-info": "echo '=====================================' && echo 'Build Information' && echo '=====================================' && echo \"Build Date: $(date)\" && echo \"🔧 Node Version: $(node --version)\" && echo \"NPM Version: $(npm --version)\" && echo '=====================================' && echo ''"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "@sap/cds": "^7",
    "express": "^4"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "jest": "^29",
    "@sap/cds-dk": "^7"
  }
}

Then, update `.sap_cid/config.yaml` to call this script:

---
stages:
  build:
    buildTool: "mta"
    buildDescriptor: "mta.yaml"
    buildToolVersion: "MBTJ21N22"
    runFirst:
      command: "npm run build-info"
  malwareScan:
    scan: false
  additionalTests:
    npmTests:
      buildDescriptor: "package.json"
      npmScript: "test"

How it works:

  • `runFirst` executes commands at the beginning of the build stage (before MTA build)
  • We call `npm run build-info` which executes our custom script from package.json
  • The script displays useful build information (date, Node version, npm version)
  • Use `&&` to chain multiple commands - each subsequent command only executes if the preceding one succeeds

2.9: Commit Changes and Test

git add .sap_cid/config.yaml package.json
git commit -m "Add custom build-info script to pipeline"
git push

Run the job in CI/CD service.

Screenshot 2.12: Build information output in logs.

2.10: Advanced Configuration Options

Here are some additional configuration options you can explore, I would recommend you to check the following guides for this:

  • Configure a Cloud Foundry Environment Job in Your Repository (Stages Configuration Repository section)
  • Adding Additional Commands to Stages

2.11: Troubleshooting Configuration Issues

Build fails after switching to Source Repository:

  • Verify `.sap_cid/config.yaml` exists in repository root
  • Check YAML syntax (use a YAML validator)
  • Ensure all referenced npm scripts exist in package.json

Configuration changes not applied:

  • Make sure changes are committed and pushed to Git
  • Verify the job is in "Source Repository" mode
  • Check the branch name matches the job configuration

"Configuration file not found" error:

  • File must be named exactly `.sap_cid/config.yaml`
  • File must be in the repository root
  • Case-sensitive on Linux systems

---

Part 3: Webhook Automation & Event-Driven CI/CD

Manual builds are great for learning, but the real power of CI/CD comes from automation. Let's set up webhooks to trigger builds automatically when you push code.

3.1: Understanding Webhooks

Webhooks are HTTP callbacks that Git providers (GitHub, GitLab, etc.) send to SAP CI/CD service when events occur in your repository.

Common webhook events:

  • Push to branch
  • Pull request created/updated
  • Tag created
  • Branch created/deleted

3.2: Create Webhook Credential and Get Webhook Data

Before we can set up the webhook, we need to create a webhook credential in SAP CI/CD and get the webhook information.

Step 1: Navigate to Repository Settings

  1. In SAP Continuous Integration and Delivery, go to the Repositories tab
  2. Click on your repository (`cicd-service`)
  3. The detail view opens with a Webhook Event Receiver section

Step 2: Create Webhook Credential

In the Webhook Event Receiver section:

  1. Type: Select GitHub from the dropdown
  2. Webhook Credential: Open the matchcode, and then click on the + (plus) icon to create a new credential
  3. A dialog opens for creating the credential:
  • Name: Enter a name (e.g., `github-webhook-secret`)
  • Description: Optional description (e.g., "Webhook secret for GitHub integration")
  • Secret: Click Generate to automatically generate a secure secret. IMPORTANT: Copy the generated secret immediately! You'll need it for GitHub and won't be able to see it again
  1. Click Create to save the credential, and click on save the repository we were modifying.

Step 3: Get Webhook Data

After creating the credential, the Webhook Event Receiver section now shows:

  • Payload URL - The webhook URL for GitHub
  • Content Type - `application/json`
  • Webhook Credential - The credential you just created. As said before, now you cannot see it anymore.

Screenshot 3.5: Complete webhook data

Keep this information ready - you'll need it for the next step

3.3: Configure Webhook in GitHub

Now let's configure the webhook in your GitHub repository using the information from SAP CI/CD:

  1. Go to your GitHub repository
  2. Click on the Settings tab (repository settings, not account settings)
  3. From the left navigation pane, choose Webhooks
  4. Click Add webhook

Configure the webhook with the information from SAP CI/CD:

  • Payload URL: Paste the Payload URL from the Webhook Event Receiver section
  • Content type: Select `application/json`
  • Secret: Paste the secret you copied when creating the Webhook Credential
  • Which events would you like to trigger this webhook?
  • Select Just the push event
  • Active: Ensure this checkbox is selected

Screenshot 3.7: Webhook configuration form with SAP CI/CD data

Click Add webhook.

3.4: Test Webhook with a Code Change

Let's make a small change to trigger the webhook:

Update `srv/hello-service.js`:

module.exports = (srv) => {
    srv.on('sayHello', () => {
        return 'Hello World from SAP Build Code CI/CD! 🚀';
    });
};

Also change the test!

Commit and push:

git add srv/hello-service.js
git commit -m "Add rocket emoji to greeting message"
git push

3.5: Automatic Build Triggered

Within seconds, check your SAP CI/CD service - a new build should automatically start.

Screenshot 3.10: Build automatically triggered by webhook

3.6: Webhook Delivery History

You can verify webhook delivery in GitHub:

  1. Go to your repository's Settings → Webhooks
  2. Click on your webhook
  3. Click Recent Deliveries tab

Screenshot 3.12: Webhook delivery history in GitHub

3.7: Configure Scheduled/Timed Builds

Besides event-driven builds, you can also schedule builds to run periodically (e.g., nightly builds).

In SAP CI/CD job configuration:

  1. Edit your job
  2. Go to Triggers tab
  3. Enable Scheduled Builds
  4. Configure cron pattern

Example cron patterns:

  • 0 2 * * * - Every day at 2 AM
  • 0 0 * * 0 - Every Sunday at midnight
  • 0 */6 * * * - Every 6 hours

Screenshot 3.14: Scheduled build configuration

3.8: Branch-Specific Webhook Triggers

You can configure jobs to only trigger for specific branches:

In Job Configuration:

  • Branch: Use wildcards like `feature/*` or `release/*`
  • Multiple jobs can listen to the same repository on different branches

---

Part 4: Multi-Branch Strategies & Best Practices

Modern development workflows use feature branches for parallel development. Let's configure our CI/CD pipeline to handle multiple branches effectively.

4.1: Understanding Multi-Branch Workflows

Common branching strategies:

GitFlow:

  • `main` - Production code
  • `develop` - Integration branch
  • `feature/*` - Feature development
  • `release/*` - Release preparation
  • `hotfix/*` - Production fixes

GitHub Flow (simpler):

  • `main` - Production code
  • `feature/*` - All development branches

4.2: Create a Feature Branch

Let's create a feature branch for our application:

# Create and switch to feature branch
git checkout -b feature/add-goodbye-endpoint

# Verify current branch
git branch

4.3: Add New Feature to Application

Let's add a new goodbye endpoint to demonstrate multi-branch development:

Update `srv/hello-service.cds`:

service HelloService {
    function sayHello() returns String;
    function sayGoodbye() returns String;
}

Update `srv/hello-service.js`:

module.exports = (srv) => {
    srv.on('sayHello', () => {
        return 'Hello World from SAP Build Code CI/CD! 🚀';
    });

    srv.on('sayGoodbye', () => {
        return 'Goodbye! Thanks for using SAP Build Code CI/CD! 👋';
    });
};

Add test for new endpoint (`tests/hello-service.test.js`):

const cds = require('@sap/cds/lib');
const path = require('path');

describe('Hello Service', () => {
    let srv;

    beforeAll(async () => {
        const csn = await cds.load(path.join(__dirname, '../srv/hello-service.cds'));
        await cds.deploy(csn);
        srv = await cds.serve('HelloService').from(csn);
    });

    test('sayHello returns correct message', async () => {
        const result = await srv.tx().send('sayHello');
        expect(result).toBe('Hello World from SAP Build Code CI/CD! 🚀');
    });

    test('sayGoodbye returns correct message', async () => {
        const result = await srv.tx().send('sayGoodbye');
        expect(result).toBe('Goodbye! Thanks for using SAP Build Code CI/CD! 👋');
    });
});

Test locally:

npm test

Screenshot 4.2: Tests passing with new feature

4.4: Commit and Push Feature Branch

git add .
git commit -m "Add goodbye endpoint to HelloService"
git push -u origin feature/add-goodbye-endpoint

Screenshot 4.3: Pushing feature branch

4.5: Create Job for Feature Branches

Now let's create a CI/CD job that automatically builds all feature branches:

  1. In SAP CI/CD, go to Jobs
  2. Click + (Create Job)
  3. Configure:
  • Job Name: `cicd-service-features`
  • Repository: `cicd-service`
  • Branch:`feature/*` (using wildcard)
  • Pipeline: Cloud Foundry Environment
  • Configuration Mode: Repository Configuration

Screenshot 4.4: Creating multi-branch job with wildcard

4.6: Automatic Build for Feature Branch

The webhook automatically triggers a build for the feature branch in the next pushes to remote.

Screenshot 4.6: Feature branch build triggered

4.7: Branch-Specific Configurations

You can customize pipeline behavior per branch pattern. For feature branches, you might want a simpler configuration that skips deployment stages.

4.8: Build Retention Policies

Configure how long to keep build artifacts:

  1. Edit job
  2. Configure Build Retention:
  • Max. Builds: 50 builds
  • Keep Builds for: 7 days

Screenshot 4.9: Build retention configuration

This prevents storage bloat from feature branch builds.

4.9: Multiple Branches in Action

Let's create another feature branch to see multiple parallel builds:

# Create another feature
git checkout main
git checkout -b feature/add-timestamp

# Make a change
echo "// Feature: add timestamp" >> srv/hello-service.js

# Commit and push
git add .
git commit -m "Add timestamp feature placeholder"
git push -u origin feature/add-timestamp

Screenshot 4.10: Multiple feature branches building

4.11: Merge Feature to Main

Once feature is complete and CI passes:

# Switch to main
git checkout main

# Merge feature
git merge feature/add-goodbye-endpoint

# Push to main
git push

This triggers the main branch job automatically.

4.12: Multi-Branch Best Practices

Job Configuration:

  • Separate jobs for `main` vs `feature/*` branches
  • More thorough testing on main branch
  • Skip deployment stages for feature branches
  • Use retention policies to clean up old builds

Branch Strategy:

  • Use consistent naming (feature/*, bugfix/*, hotfix/*)
  • Keep feature branches short-lived
  • Require CI success before merging
  • Delete branches after merging

Configuration:

  • Share common config, override when needed
  • Use branch-specific configs for different behaviors
  • Document branch patterns in a README file

---

Part 5: GitHub Actions Integration - Pull Request Status Checks

While webhooks trigger SAP CI/CD builds automatically, wouldn't it be great if you could see the build status directly in your GitHub Pull Requests? In this section, we'll integrate SAP CI/CD build status with GitHub's PR checks, providing real-time feedback to developers.

5.1: The Integration Challenge

When you create a Pull Request on GitHub, it doesn't automatically know about your SAP CI/CD build status. GitHub shows its own Actions results, but the actual build happening in SAP CI/CD is invisible to the PR workflow. Knowing the status of the tests if they are passing or not, if the image is being built properly or not, if the malware scan detected some anomalous thing, might be interesting prior a merge to main branch with all the code.

What we want to achieve:

  • Automatic build status checks on Pull Requests
  • Block merging if SAP CI/CD build fails
  • Show build URL for easy debugging
  • Wait for build completion and report results

5.2: Enable API Access in SAP BTP

Step 1: Enable API access to SAP CI/CD service. This allows GitHub Actions to query build status programmatically.

Step 2: Create Service Key

  1. Click on your service CI/CD instance
  2. Go to Service Keys tab
  3. Click Create Service Key -> Service Key Name: `cicd-github-actions`
  4. Click Create

Step 3: Copy API Credentials

Click View on your service key to see the credentials. Keep this in hand, we are going to use them for the github integration.

5.3: Configure GitHub Secrets

Now we'll store the API credentials securely in GitHub Secrets.

⚠️ Importnat: GitHub may truncate secrets that contain special characters (or at least this happened to me while configuring this). The `client secret` from the service often contains special characters that can cause authentication failures.

Solution: Base64 encode the client secret before storing it in GitHub.

Step 1: Base64 Encode the Client Secret

# Replace YOUR_CLIENT_SECRET with the actual secret from service key
echo -n "YOUR_CLIENT_SECRET" | base64

Copy the output - this is your base64-encoded secret.

Step 2: Add Secrets to GitHub

  1. Go to your GitHub repository
  2. Click Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions
  3. Click New repository secret

Add the following secrets:

Secret 1: Client ID

  • Name: `SAP_CICD_CLIENT_ID`
  • Value: Your clientid from service key

Secret 2: Client Secret (Base64 Encoded)

  • Name: `SAP_CICD_CLIENT_SECRET_BASE64`
  • Value: The base64-encoded secret from Step 1

Secret 3: Token URL

  • Name: `SAP_CICD_TOKEN_URL`
  • Value: The OAuth URL from service key + `/oauth/token`
  • Example: `https://your-subdomain.authentication.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token`

Secret 4: API URL

  • Name: `SAP_CICD_API_URL`
  • Value: The ci/cd service endpoint from service key

Secret 5: Job Name

  • Name: `SAP_CICD_JOB_NAME`
  • Value: Your CI/CD job name (e.g., `cicd-service-main`)

5.4: Create GitHub Actions Workflow

Now let's create a GitHub Actions workflow that checks SAP CI/CD build status for Pull Requests.

Create `.github/workflows/sap-cicd-status.yml`:

name: SAP CI/CD Build Status Check

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize, reopened]
    branches:
      - main

jobs:
  check-sap-cicd-build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    name: Check SAP CI/CD Build Status

    steps:
        - name: Get PR Commit SHA
          id: pr-info
          run: |
            echo "commit_sha=${{ github.event.pull_request.head.sha }}" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
            echo "branch=${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
            echo "🔍 PR Commit SHA: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.sha }}"
            echo "🌿 Branch: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}"

        - name: Get OAuth Token
          id: get-token
          run: |
            echo "🔐 Getting OAuth token..."

            # Decode base64 secret to handle special characters
            CLIENT_ID="${{ secrets.SAP_CICD_CLIENT_ID }}"
            CLIENT_SECRET=$(echo "${{ secrets.SAP_CICD_CLIENT_SECRET_BASE64 }}" | base64 -d)

            echo "Client ID length: ${#CLIENT_ID}"
            echo "Client Secret length: ${#CLIENT_SECRET}"

            # Use Basic Authentication
            response=$(curl -s -X POST "${{ secrets.SAP_CICD_TOKEN_URL }}" \
              -u "${CLIENT_ID}:${CLIENT_SECRET}" \
              -d "grant_type=client_credentials")

            token=$(echo "$response" | jq -r '.access_token')
            if [ "$token" == "null" ] || [ -z "$token" ]; then
              echo "❌ Failed to get OAuth token"
              echo "Response: $response"
              exit 1
            fi

            echo "✅ OAuth token obtained"
            echo "token=$token" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

        - name: Find Build for Commit
          id: find-build
          run: |
            echo "🔍 Looking for SAP CI/CD build for commit ${{ steps.pr-info.outputs.commit_sha }}..."

            # Try to find the build (with retries in case it hasn't started yet)
            max_attempts=10
            attempt=0
            build_found=false

            while [ $attempt -lt $max_attempts ]; do
              attempt=$((attempt + 1))
              echo "Attempt $attempt/$max_attempts..."

              response=$(curl -s -X GET \
                "${{ secrets.SAP_CICD_API_URL }}/v2/jobs/${{ secrets.SAP_CICD_JOB_NAME }}/builds?filter=latest" \
                -H "Authorization: Bearer ${{ steps.get-token.outputs.token }}")

              # Extract commit hash from the latest build
              commit_hash=$(echo $response | jq -r '._embedded.builds[0].builtCommit.hash')
              build_status=$(echo $response | jq -r '._embedded.builds[0].status')
              build_result=$(echo $response | jq -r '._embedded.builds[0].result')
              build_number=$(echo $response | jq -r '._embedded.builds[0].number')
              build_url=$(echo $response | jq -r '._embedded.builds[0]._links.ui.href')

              echo "Found build #$build_number for commit: $commit_hash"
              echo "Status: $build_status | Result: $build_result"

              if [ "$commit_hash" == "${{ steps.pr-info.outputs.commit_sha }}" ]; then
                echo "✅ Found matching build!"
                build_found=true
                echo "build_status=$build_status" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
                echo "build_result=$build_result" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
                echo "build_number=$build_number" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
                echo "build_url=$build_url" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
                break
              fi

              echo "⏳ Build not found yet, waiting 10 seconds..."
              sleep 10
            done

            if [ "$build_found" = false ]; then
              echo "❌ Could not find build for commit ${{ steps.pr-info.outputs.commit_sha }}"
              exit 1
            fi

        - name: Wait for Build Completion
          id: wait-build
          if: steps.find-build.outputs.build_status != 'FINISHED'
          run: |
            echo "⏳ Build is not finished yet. Waiting for completion..."

            max_wait=1800  # 30 minutes
            elapsed=0
            interval=30

            while [ $elapsed -lt $max_wait ]; do
              response=$(curl -s -X GET \
                "${{ secrets.SAP_CICD_API_URL }}/v2/jobs/${{ secrets.SAP_CICD_JOB_NAME }}/builds/${{ steps.find-build.outputs.build_number }}" \
                -H "Authorization: Bearer ${{ steps.get-token.outputs.token }}")

              status=$(echo $response | jq -r '.status')
              result=$(echo $response | jq -r '.result')

              echo "Status: $status | Result: $result"

              if [ "$status" == "FINISHED" ]; then
                echo "✅ Build completed!"
                echo "final_status=$status" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
                echo "final_result=$result" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
                break
              fi

              echo "Still running... (${elapsed}s elapsed)"
              sleep $interval
              elapsed=$((elapsed + interval))
            done

            if [ $elapsed -ge $max_wait ]; then
              echo "⚠️  Timeout waiting for build to complete"
              exit 1
            fi

        - name: Check Build Result
          run: |
            # Use the final result if we waited, otherwise use the found result
            result="${{ steps.wait-build.outputs.final_result }}"
            if [ -z "$result" ] || [ "$result" == "null" ]; then
              result="${{ steps.find-build.outputs.build_result }}"
            fi

            echo "📊 Final Build Result: $result"
            echo "🔗 Build URL: ${{ steps.find-build.outputs.build_url }}"

            # Check for successful build results
            if [ "$result" == "SUCCESS" ] || [ "$result" == "FINISHED" ]; then
              echo "✅ SAP CI/CD build passed!"
              exit 0
            else
              echo "❌ SAP CI/CD build failed with result: $result"
              echo ""
              echo "Please check the build details at:"
              echo "${{ steps.find-build.outputs.build_url }}"
              exit 1
            fi

5.5: How It Works

Let's break down what this workflow does:

Step 1: Get PR Commit SHA

  • Extracts the commit hash from the Pull Request
  • This is used to match against SAP CI/CD builds

Step 2: Get OAuth Token

  • Obtains OAuth access token for API calls

Step 3: Find Build for Commit

  • Polls SAP CI/CD API for the latest build
  • Matches the commit SHA with the PR commit
  • Retries up to 10 times (builds might not start immediately)
  • Extracts build status, result, number, and URL

Step 4: Wait for Build Completion

  • If build is still running, polls every 30 seconds
  • Maximum wait time: 30 minutes
  • Updates when build status becomes "FINISHED"

Step 5: Check Build Result

  • Evaluates the final build result
  • Provides build URL for debugging failures
  • Exits with error code if build fails

5.6: Commit and Test the Workflow

# Add the workflow file
git add .github/workflows/sap-cicd-status.yml

# Commit
git commit -m "Add GitHub Actions integration for SAP CI/CD build status"

# Push to main
git push

5.7: Test with a Pull Request

Let's test the integration by creating a Pull Request:

# Create a feature branch
git checkout -b feature/test-github-integration

# Make a small change
echo "// Test GitHub integration" >> srv/hello-service.js

# Commit and push
git add srv/hello-service.js
git commit -m "Test: GitHub Actions integration"
git push -u origin feature/test-github-integration

Now create a Pull Request on GitHub targeting the `main` branch.

Screenshot 5.6: Pull Request with status check running

5.8: Watch the Magic Happen

The workflow will:

  1. Trigger automatically when PR is created
  2. Wait for SAP CI/CD build to start
  3. Poll build status until completion
  4. Report results back to the PR

Screenshot 5.7: PR status check successful

5.9: Build Failure Scenario

If the SAP CI/CD build fails (e.g., tests fail, build errors), the GitHub status check will fail and block merging.

The workflow provides the build URL so developers can investigate:

❌ SAP CI/CD build failed with result: FAILURE Please check the build details at: https://cicd-service.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/ui/index.html#/builds/123

5.10: Advanced Configurations

- Configure Branch Protection Rules

To enforce build status before merging:

  1. Go to Repository Settings → Branches
  2. Add rule for `main` branch
  3. Enable Require status checks to pass before merging
  4. Select Check SAP CI/CD Build Status

- Customize Timeout and Retry Values

Adjust polling behavior in the workflow:

# In "Find Build for Commit"
step max_attempts=10 # Number of retries to find
build sleep 10 # Seconds between retries

# In "Wait for Build Completion"
step max_wait=1800 # Maximum wait time (30 minutes)
interval=30 # Poll interval (30 seconds)

- Add Status Badges to your README file

Add a status badge showing CI/CD status in your README.md:

[![SAP CI/CD Status](https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/YOUR-REPO/workflows/SAP%20CI%2FCD%20Build%20Status%20Check/badge.svg)](https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/YOUR-REPO/actions)

5.12: Benefits of This Integration

Developer Experience:

  • See build status directly in Pull Requests
  • Block merging of broken code automatically
  • Quick access to build logs via provided URL
  • No need to switch between GitHub and SAP CI/CD UI

Team Benefits:

  • Enforce quality gates before merge
  • Reduce manual review overhead
  • Catch issues earlier in development cycle
  • Maintain code quality standards

DevOps Benefits:

  • Single source of truth for build status
  • Automated enforcement of CI/CD checks
  • Audit trail of all build results
  • Integration with GitHub's native PR workflow

---

Conclusion & Next Steps

Congratulations! You've successfully set up a comprehensive CI/CD pipeline for your SAP CAP application using SAP Build Code's Continuous Integration & Delivery service, complete with GitHub Actions integration!

What We've Accomplished

In this guide, we covered:

  • Part 1: Set up first CI/CD pipeline with a simple CAP application
  • Part 2: Mastered advanced configuration with repository-based config
  • Part 3: Automated builds with webhooks and event-driven triggers
  • Part 4: Implemented multi-branch strategies for parallel development
  • Part 5: Integrated SAP CI/CD build status with GitHub Pull Requests

Personal Thoughts

In today's competitive landscape, speed of delivery isn't just an advantage, it's a necessity. Automation is the key to
staying agile. The more you automate, the faster you can deliver enhancements and new features to your users.

I strongly encourage you to implement this CI/CD setup and evaluate which projects in your portfolio could benefit from
it. The return on investment is substantial, by automating your deployment pipeline, you eliminate significant
operational overhead and free your team to focus on what truly matters: writing correct, secure, and functional code.
Let the CI/CD service handle the heavy lifting of cloud deployments.

SAP's documentation for the Continuous Integration & Delivery service is comprehensive and includes helpful video
tutorials that guide you through the configuration process step-by-step. Getting started is easier than you might
think.

Stop deploying manually. Start shipping smarter. Your future self (and your team) will thank you.

I am thinking about a post of integrating this service with Cloud Transport Management Service in BTP. I will start considering it and working on it.

See you in the next blog!

Nata.

entry.title: What’s new in Mobile development kit client 26.3
entry.content:
Dear Community,

I'm happy to announce that a new release of the Mobile Development Kit (MDK) is now available for all SAP Build and Mobile Services customers. You can download it from the SAP Software Center, and it will soon be available on the community download page.

The SAP Mobile Services Client has been updated to version 26.3.0 on the Apple App Store and the Google Play Store.

SAP MDK enables developers and technical business users to build cross-platform mobile applications. It allows you to build your application once, in an integrated development environment (SAP Business Application Studio / Visual Studio Code) and run it natively on Mobile (iOS & Android).  

Below UI enhancements are available only on iOS and Android platforms:

  • Support Styling of Key Value Item Control
  • Contact Cell Control Enhancements
  • Support In-App Camera in FormCell Attachment Control (Android Only)
  • New ActionBar Caption Size Options
  • Support Required Field Indicator for Segmented FormCell Control
  • Support Uploading Offline Store to Mobile Services
  • Onboarding via MDM App Config (Managed Configuration)

Support Styling of Key Value Item Control

MDK now supports styling Key Value item control. A new object property Styles has been introduced with following sub-properties:

  • KeyValueItem: supports only background color to style entire item.
  • KeyName: supports to style key name.
  • Value: supports to style a value property.
//excerpt of an MDK page
"KeyAndValues": [{
        "Value": "{City}",
        "_Type": "KeyValue.Type.Item",
        "_Name": "KeyValue0",
        "KeyName": "City",
        "Visible": true,
        "OnPress": "/MDK263/Actions/UpdateEntitySuccessMessage.action",
        "LinkColor": "#0000FF",
        "Styles": {
            "KeyValueItem": "KeyValueItemBackground",
            "KeyName": "KeyNameStyle",
            "Value": "KeyValueStyle"
        }
    },

//excerpt of a .less file 
.KeyValueItemBackground {
    background-color: #FFA500;
}
.KeyNameStyle {
    font-color: #ADD8E6;
    text-decoration: underline;
}
.KeyValueStyle {
    font-color: green;
}

Note:  : Value  style will take priority over LinkColor, regardless of whether OnPress is defined or not.

 

Key Value Styling on iOS and Andrid devices

 For more information, refer to the Key Value Item documentation.

Contact Cell Control Enhancements

  • Support for Initials: The ContactCell component now supports displaying user initials, providing a more personalized and recognizable visual experience when profile pictures are unavailable. This enhancement makes it easier to scan and identify contacts by replacing generic placeholder icons with meaningful initials. Three new properties have been introduced to support this feature:
    • DetailImageText: A string property that displays text in the image container when no DetailImage is present. It supports a maximum of 2–4 characters, allowing you to manually specify custom text such as initials.
    • DetailImageIsCircular: A boolean property with a default value of true that controls the shape of the image container. When set to true, the image is displayed in a circular shape; when set to false, it is displayed in a rounded rectangular shape.
    • UseHeadlineForDetailImage: A boolean property with a default value of false. When set to true and no DetailImage is provided, the control automatically extracts and displays the first two initials from the Headline property in the image container. This property takes precedence over DetailImageText.
  • Support for BadgeImage Property:  A new property, BadgeImage, is now available on ContactCell. This property allows you to attach an additional indicator icon or image to the existing DetailImage or DetailImageText for each contact. For example, it can be used to highlight and identify primary contacts in a contact list. Additionally, the existing Styles property for ContactCellItem now supports a new subproperty named BadgeImage, which enables CSS styling for the indicator icon or image defined in the BadgeImage property.
//excerpt of .page
{
    "ContactCell": {
        "_Name": "ContactCellItem6",
        "BadgeImage": "sap-icon://bell",
        "DetailImageText": "SB",
        "DetailImageIsCircular": true,
        "Headline": "Sue Bay",
        "Subheadline": "Antioch, Illinois",
        "UseHeadlineForDetailImage": false,
        "Visible": true,
        "ActivityItems": [{
                "_Name": "ContactCellItem4ActivityItems0",
                "ActivityType": "Phone"
            }
        ],
        "Styles": {
            "DetailImage": "DetailImage",
            "BadgeImage": "BadgeImage"
        }
    }
},

//excerpt of Styles.less
.BadgeImage {
    color: #068831;
    background-color: #ffae00;
}
.DetailImage {
    color: #AD00FF;
    background-color: #ADD8E6;
}

Contact Cell Control on iOS and Android devices

For more information, refer to the Contact Cell documentation.

Support In-App Camera in FormCell Attachment Control (Android Only)

We have received feedback from customers about the MDK app restarting after taking a photo using the Attachment control, especially when the app is running on Android devices with lower RAM. This may be due to memory pressure on the device, which can cause the OS to kill the MDK app running on background after launching the camera.

To address this problem, we are providing support for using an in-app camera option. This option consumes less memory, and the camera opens directly inside the MDK app instead of switching to a separate camera application.

  • A new UseInAppCamera property is available in the Application.app file under the Settings object. It determines whether the in-app camera or the device's default camera will be used for the AttachmentFormCell control’s “Take Photo” option.
    • When set to true, the in-app camera will be used instead of launching the device’s native camera application.
//excerpt of Application.app
{
    "MainPage": "/MDK263/Pages/Main.page",
    "OnLaunch": [
        "/MDK263/Rules/Service/Initialize.js"
    ],
    "Settings": {
        "UseInAppCamera": true
    },
    "_Name": "MDK263"
}

In addition, we are introducing two new APIs to manage the in-app camera settings programmatically:

  • getUseInAppCamera 
    • Retrieves the current state of the in-app camera usage flag.
    • Returns the current value determining whether the in-app camera is enabled for FormCell Attachment controls.
  • setUseInAppCamera 
    • Enables or disables the in-app camera for FormCell Attachment controls
    • Changes take effect immediately for new AttachmentFormCell instances
    • For AttachmentFormCell controls that are already rendered on the current page, the new setting will only take effect after calling the control’s reset() API. Calling the reset() API will discard any existing attachments in the control.
export default function ChangeUseInAppCamera(context) {
  let currentValue = context.getUseInAppCamera();
  context.setUseInAppCamera(!currentValue);
  alert('UseInAppCamera set to ' + !currentValue);
  let currentPageProxy = context.getPageProxy();
  if (currentPageProxy) {
    if (currentPageProxy.getName() === 'FCMain') {
      let attachmentControlProxy = context.evaluateTargetPathForAPI('#Page:FCMain/#Control:FormCellAttachment0');
      if (attachmentControlProxy) {
        attachmentControlProxy.reset();
      }
    }
  }
}

New ActionBar Caption Size Options

The ActionBar now supports flexible caption sizing through the new PreferredCaptionSize property, which replaces the existing PrefersLargeCaption property.

  • Enhanced size control: Choose from small, medium, or large caption sizes instead of toggling between only two options.
    • iOS supports small and large caption size
    • Android supports small, medium, and large caption size.

Behavior Notes:        

  • When PreferredCaptionSize is set to "large", any defined Subhead will be ignored.
  • When setting CaptionAlignment (Android only) to “center”, the PreferCaptionSize automatically set to small.  
  • On iOS, large caption mode is not supported on pages with BottomNavigation, Tabs, SideDrawer, FormCellContainer, or FlexibleColumn controls.
  • If both PreferredCaptionSize and the deprecated PrefersLargeCaption property are specified, PreferredCaptionSize takes precedence.
//excerpt of an MDK page 
"ActionBar": {
        "Items": [
            {
                "_Type": "Control.Type.ActionBarItem",
                "_Name": "ActionBarItem0",
                "Caption": "User Menu",
                "OnPress": "/MDK263/Actions/Application/UserMenuPopover.action"
            }
        ],
        "_Name": "ActionBar1",
        "_Type": "Control.Type.ActionBar",
        "Caption": "MDK 26.3",
        "PreferredCaptionSize": "Medium"
    }
}

 

Support Required Field Indicator  for Segmented FormCell Control 

In MDK 25.9 release, we introduced support for required field indicators for some FormCell controls. In this release, we are extending this support to the Segmented FormCell, indicating that the field must be filled out.
Note: This feature does not enforce users to fill out the form cell; it only provides a visual indicator that the field is required.

//Excerpt from a .page
{
    "RequiredIndicator": true,
    "_Type": "Control.Type.FormCell.SegmentedControl",
    "_Name": "PrioritySegmented",
    "Caption": "Priority",
    "ApportionsSegmentWidthsByContent": false,
    "Segments": [
        "Low",
        "Medium",
        "High"
    ]
}

Form Cell Segmented control on iOS and Android devices

 

Support Uploading Offline Store to Mobile Services 

MDK now provides the ability for applications to upload the offline store (Both the main offline database (UDB) and the Request Queue database) to Mobile Services to assist with troubleshooting. This capability helps developers and support teams more effectively investigate and diagnose issues related to the offline store, reducing the time required to identify the root cause of problems reported by business users.
To facilitate this capability, a new MDK action has been introduced: Action.Type.OfflineOData.UploadStore.

{
    "_Type": "Action.Type.OfflineOData.UploadStore",
    "ActionResult": {
        "_Name": "UploadOfflineDB"
    },
    "Service": "/MDK263/Services/SampleServiceV4.service",
    "EncryptionKey": "MDK123", // encryption key used during the upload process
    "Note": "My Test App" // Optional note attached to the uploaded database file
}

In order to use this feature, you will need to first enable offline store upload policy in SAP Mobile Services Admin UI for a given MDK app. Developers can download the uploaded files on their local machine and use ILODATA tool (link to doc) to analyze them.  (link how to use this tool). For more information, see this  documentation.

Onboarding via MDM App Config (Managed Configuration)

MDK now supports onboarding via MDM App Config (Managed Configuration). Company administrators can push onboarding settings directly to managed devices through their MDM solution.

The MDK client retrieves MDK-defined configurations from a reserved root property named MDKSettings within the Managed Configuration. Currently, this root property only accepts the ConnectionSettings object. The supported properties, their functionality, and the required structure for ConnectionSettings remain the same as in the existing implementation.

While the iOS and Android versions differ syntactically due to their file formats, they share the same structural hierarchy. Note that the actual syntax may vary depending on the requirements of the specific MDM solution being used.

  • iOS: MDM solutions for iOS commonly use an XML format for complex configurations.
  • Android: MDM solutions for Android commonly use a JSON format for complex configurations.

Become an SAP MDK Explorer!
Do you have a requirement to build a mobile solution for your enterprise? Take your first steps today by following these tutorials to get started and learn more about the SAP Mobile development kit. We’re excited to see what you build—your feedback and ideas are invaluable!

Submit Improvement Requests
You can submit your improvement request (link) for Mobile development kit to the SAP Customer Influence site, a central place for all product improvement requests. You can browse all improvement requests, submit improvement requests, comment, vote, receive updates, and see who has voted at the SAP Customer Influence site.

entry.title: SAP Fiori Tools 2603 Update: RAP Project Accelerator, Walkthrough, Adaptation Improvements, and More
entry.content:
With SAP Fiori tools 2603, we deliver a set of focused improvements across development transparency, onboarding, AI-supported development, and extensibility. Highlights include Project Accelerator support for RAP, a change in navigation from properties to its source, a new walkthrough in Visual Studio Code, and the ability to import existing key user changes into an SAPUI5 Adaptation Project.

Project Accelerator for RAP generates full-stack SAP Fiori elements applications from text or images

One of the most important additions in this release is the extension of the Project Accelerator to the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP). The Project Accelerator has been available for SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) scenarios since 2024 and supports rapid prototyping with AI, based on images and/or a requirements description. With release 2603, it can now also create ABAP artifacts and services with AI assistance, addressing a long-standing request.

This significantly broadens the reach of the Project Accelerator for enterprise SAP development. The feature is available for cloud-based RAP back ends with SAP BTP or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602 or higher. For more information please refer to the SAP Help Portal page Project Accelerator Using SAP Fiori Tools AI. A separate blog post dedicated to this addition is currently in preparation and will be linked here once published.

The Project Accelerator lets you generate applications directly from business requirements documents, either text or images.

 

 

 

 

The Project Accelerator for RAP builds on the capabilities already available for CAP.

 

Direct navigation to source in the manifest gives developers a more transparent editing experience

Since the beginning, SAP Fiori tools has followed a clear principle: support development tasks with convenience, but do not hide the underlying technical details from experienced developers. This is why properties and settings in the tooling generally allow navigation to the corresponding source. For annotations and XML-based settings, this already opens the relevant annotation or fragment file directly. For manifest-related changes, however, SAP Fiori tools previously used a simplified virtual representation of the manifest as an intermediate editing layer.

Over time, this abstraction became more of a liability than a benefit. It introduced additional complexity, made parts of the implementation more error-prone, and did not provide enough added value compared to working directly with the actual manifest.json. With release 2603, source links for manifest properties now open the corresponding location in the real manifest and highlight the relevant section directly. This gives developers a more transparent editing experience and simplifies the path from visual tooling to source code. It also creates room for future optimizations in areas such as undo/redo handling and patch logic, which are planned for a later release.

New walkthrough for getting started makes novice developers more productive more quickly

Users who already work with SAP Fiori tools often appreciate the convenience and breadth of what the tooling offers. At the same time, the number of available capabilities can make it harder for newcomers to find a good starting point. To make onboarding easier, we have added a new walkthrough in Visual Studio Code that helps users get started with SAP Fiori tools directly inside the IDE.

The walkthrough provides a practical entry point into the most relevant concepts and flows. It guides users through the initial setup and encourages hands-on exploration of the tooling. If you are new to SAP Fiori tools, or if you want a structured way to revisit the basics, try it out and complete the tasks.

The walkthrough can be started from the Help menu in VS Code, choose Open Walkthrough… and then SAP Fiori tools from the drop-down menu.

The SAP Fiori tools walkthrough explains all the capabilities and has hands-on opportunities to explore and learn.

Import key user changes into an Adaptation Project and keep your edits

With this release, when creating an SAPUI5 Adaptation Project, you can now import existing key user changes. This helps ensure that changes made through key user adaptation are not lost when moving to developer extensibility. The feature is available starting with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602.

For more background, read about key user adaptation and the transition to developer extensibility.

Additional new features in SAP Fiori tools 2603 and more planned

Release 2603 includes an update of the tools and generator to support ESLint v9. In parallel, we are internally testing a new validation feature that allows more flexible, domain-specific rules and visual integration of fix options into the Page Map and Page Editor. We plan to release this together with an initial ruleset in one of the upcoming SAP Fiori tools releases and cover it in a separate blog post.

There is also an improved support for value help services referenced by an app’s main OData V4 service. Their metadata can now be retrieved during app generation, which enables SAP Fiori tools to support scenarios such as property configuration for these value helps and mock data creation for preview. This work is part of a broader rework of test generation support that we plan to cover in a future release blog.

The MCP server for SAP Fiori can now generate an SAP Fiori elements application for an existing OData service, including services exposed from a RAP back end. It uses saved system connections from the Connection Manager extension together with a system-relative service URI. This means that the authentication flow can remain outside the AI client and the LLM, following the established SAP system sign-in flow. This is one step toward end-to-end, AI-driven, RAP-based application development in Visual Studio Code, and we will continue to expand this path in upcoming releases.

If you want to stay up to date with changes in SAP Fiori tools between release blog posts, the What’s New section in the SAP Help Portal remains the best place to track newly delivered capabilities and fixes.

The SAP Fiori development portal includes explanations and sample code for all the building blocks available for creating SAP Fiori elements applications.

entry.title: VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon 2026: Champions Crowned at SYNC DemoJam
entry.content:
The Stage is Set
The VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon 2026 reached its spectacular grand finale on the main stage of VNSG SYNC 2026 – the annual VNSG and SAP Netherlands year event. In front of a packed keynote audience on 12 March, three finalist teams had exactly five minutes to demonstrate the business impact of their SAP BTP-powered innovations. The audience then cast their votes – and the results were electric.

From Challenge to Champion: The Journey
The hackathon launched in late 2025 when VNSG, SAP BTP Product Management and SAP NL Presales invited Dutch SAP customers and partners to submit real-life business challenges. Six teams were selected and, after a structured enablement phase, spent weeks co-building solutions with dedicated coaches. In February 2026, all six teams pitched to a jury panel, which selected three finalists for the grand DemoJam finale at SYNC. The three finalists stood out for their clear business relevance, technical ambition and the real-world potential to take their prototypes to production.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Champion: Promocean + SOA People — Purchase Order Control Center
Luc van Elk and Thomas Langhout from Promocean, supported by SOA People, took the audience vote and claimed the top spot. Their solution – the Purchase Order Control Center – puts SAP Business AI at the heart of procurement operations.
By connecting AI directly to SAP ECC, the team demonstrated how intelligent automation can dramatically reduce manual effort, accelerate purchase order processing, and surface exceptions before they become costly problems. A showcase of how AI on SAP BTP can deliver operational excellence in one of the most common – and pain-prone – business processes in any enterprise.

Runner-up: Ecotone + Expertum — Integration Suite Management
Arjan de Mol and Bart van de Kamp from Ecotone, partnered with Expertum Consulting, earned second place with a solution that tackles a pain many integration teams know all too well: managing and controlling large numbers of iFlows across landscapes. Their Integration Suite Management solution brings Joule-powered automation to iFlow lifecycle management – enabling operators to undeploy and deploy groups of iFlows intelligently. This turns a time-consuming, error-prone manual process into a governed, AI-assisted workflow, giving integration teams transparency, stability and control. 

Third Place: NTT DATA Business Solutions — ProTrack
Laurens Steffers and Jorrit van der Sanden from NTT DATA Business Solutions presented ProTrack – a solution delivering real-time insight for machines on the production floor. Built on SAP BTP, ProTrack addresses a critical operational need in manufacturing: knowing what is happening on your shop floor right now, not minutes later. By streaming machine data into an SAP BTP-powered dashboard, teams can react faster to exceptions, reduce downtime and optimize throughput.

 

Beyond the Podium: A Strong Field
The jury had a hard job. Aarini Consulting, Nederlandse Spoorwegen (NS), and Simac IT & BSC also built
impressive solutions that demonstrated real business value on SAP BTP. Every team that reached the jury pitch proved
that you can go from business challenge to working prototype in a short time - when you combine the right platform
with the right people. A sincere congratulations to all six participating teams !!

Behind the Scenes
Want to experience the atmosphere of the DemoJam from backstage? Watch the behind-the-scenes video - including a
live demo of how SAP Business AI is changing the way people interact with SAP, presented by Jason Enti and Ronald
Schippers: Behind the scenes at the VNSG SAP BTP DemoJam SYNC 2026

The People Who Made it Happen
A hackathon like this does not happen by itself. A huge thank you to everyone who contributed:
Presented at SYNC by: Twan van den Broek and Irene Rompa
Team coaches: Jan Penninkhof , Alex van den Hoek and Twan van den Broek
Jury: Ronald Schippersm Steven Nuyts, Steffen Schad, Jonathan Freeland
Made possible by: Mientje Paais, Olaf Winkler, Judy Bui and Niklas Siemer

What This Proves
The VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon 2026 is proof that innovation does not require a blank slate or a greenfield project. It requires the right platform, the right people and a real problem worth solving.
SAP BTP – combining SAP Integration Suite, SAP Build, SAP HANA Cloud, SAP Business AI and cloud-native runtimes – gives teams everything they need to move from prototype to production at speed.
The finalist solutions were not demos for the sake of demos. They addressed real procurement pain, real integration
complexity and real manufacturing visibility gaps. And they were built in weeks, not months.

What's Next?
The conversation does not end on the SYNC stage. All teams are encouraged to work with their coaches to plan production rollouts and turn hackathon prototypes into enterprise solutions.

For the broader SAP Community : Stay tuned for the next edition of the VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon. Bring your own
real-life business challenge and co-build with partners and SAP experts.
And who knows - your solution could be on the SYNC main stage in 2027.

SAP Business Technology Platform SAP Build SAP Integration Suite SAP Business AI 

@hansvp @qmrjvd @sricsi98 @BvE @mdschoenmakers @tamasszirtes @f_van_leeuwen @Petra1 @BartvdKamp @tedcastelijns @Arjan_deMol @dvvelzen @JdTeuling @AnnikaHeus @LaurensSteffers

Related reading:
When Innovation Meets Real Business Impact – VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon 2026
Grand Finale VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon in a DemoJam

entry.title: Joule for Developers ABAP AI Sapphire and ASUG 2026 Sessions
entry.content:
In case you do not have planned your Sapphire and ASUG agenda here are my recommendations if you want to learn about the latest innovations from ABAP Platform and Joule for developers (J4D) ABAP AI capabilities:

1. Introduction to ABAP Cloud supported by generative AI | BTP1429: Get an overview on ABAP Cloud and recent innovations, get insights on Clean Core and see how generative AI boosts your adoption of ABAP Cloud conducted by Alexander Rother (VP, Head of ABAP Platform Product Management).

2. Build smarter: Agentic ABAP development tools for Visual Studio Code | BTP2573: Simona Marincei (Head of AI - SAP ABAP Platform) will show how ABAP agents orchestrate in VS Code via the ABAP MCP-server tasks, speed up coding, and boost productivity across orchestrators from various partners.

3. Agentic ABAP with SAP Joule for Developers: Build faster, migrate smarter | JOU1428: Simona Marincei (Head of AI - SAP ABAP Platform) and Nora Klemp (Senior Product Specialist) will explain how agentic ABAP accelerates SAP S/4HANA migrations and streamline ABAP Cloud development across modern and legacy environments.

4. Customer Influence and feedback: The product road map for ABAP Cloud | BTP1603: Learn from Alexander Rother (VP, Head of ABAP Platform Product Management) how to give feedback to the ABAP product roadmap with the Customer Influence site which allows submitting, tracking, and prioritizing ideas.

5. Modernize classic extensions for clean core in SAP Cloud ERP Private | BTP1426: Volker Drees (Product Expert, ABAP Cloud, SAP SE) will explain clean core extensibility practice in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. Learn how to govern clean core development using the ABAP test cockpit and how to use the ABAP Cloud development model and clean core extensibility principles to modernize your custom on-stack extensions.

 

The full Sapphire and ASUG 2026 agenda can be found here.
 
Here you can learn more:
  1. Our Roadmap for 2026 before Sapphire
  2. Your Roadmap for getting started with ABAP AI
  3. ABAP AI Revolution Accelerates: The ABAP Developer Who Built Enterprise Apps in Minutes
  4. Joule for Developers and ABAP AI capabilities are coming to SAP S/4HANA Private Edition 2021, 2022, and 2023
  5. Public Roadmap: SAP Help / SAP Roadmap Explorer
  6. Help & Guides
    1. Step-by-step guide how to activate SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities for your SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
    2. Joule for Developers (J4D) for S/4HANA Public Cloud Setup Guide
    3. SAP ABAP AI Discovery Center
    4. SAP BTP Discovery Center (Free Tier Services)
    5. Custom code migration to SAP S/4HANA powered by SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities
    6. YouTube playlist for Joule for Developers (J4D)
    7. Joule for Developers Help (J4D)
    8. Build SAP Fiori Apps with ABAP Cloud and SAP Joule for developers (RAP120) 
    9. ADT Eclipse AI Capabilities Help
    10. ABAP AI SDK Help
    11. ABAP-1 Help

More high-level insights about Joule for Developers/ABAP AI and ABAP platform can be found here . Here you find more on ABAP- 1,

entry.title: SAP HANA Cloud: Expert-Guided Implementation Workshop Series
entry.content:
SAP HANA Cloud is a fully managed, in-memory cloud database service as part of the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). It delivers high-speed processing and real-time analytics, which is especially valuable for data-intensive applications. The platform supports multiple data models, including relational, graph, spatial, JSON, and document store, allowing developers to work with different types of data in one environment. It also includes built-in data virtualization and integration capabilities, enabling organizations to access and combine data from SAP and non-SAP sources.

SAP HANA Cloud provides a comprehensive environment for building intelligent, data-driven solutions. It supports advanced analytics, machine learning, and predictive capabilities directly within the database, which reduces data movement and accelerates insight generation. Developers can use familiar tools and languages such as SQL, Java, Node.js, and Python to create applications and services.
The platform also includes data lake functionality for storing and processing large volumes of less-frequently accessed data at a lower cost, while still allowing seamless querying alongside in-memory data. Its multi-tier storage approach helps organizations balance performance and cost by automatically managing where data resides. Overall, SAP HANA Cloud enables organizations to modernize their data landscape, support real-time decision-making, and drive digital transformation initiatives.

 

🚀 Empower Your Data Journey: Introducing Our New SAP HANA Cloud Expert Guided Implementation Series 

In today’s fast-paced digital landscape, speed of transformation is a must. Businesses across the globe are evolving to make faster, smarter, and more connected decisions by building intelligent, data-driven solutions using advanced analytics, machine learning, and predictive capabilities. To support this evolution, we’re excited to announce our renewed series of Expert-Guided Implementation workshops (EGI) from SAP Enterprise Support designed to help you upskill, modernize, and future-proof your business processes. Whether you're looking to modernize legacy systems, harness the power of cloud data, or unlock real-time insights through analytics, this workshop series offers targeted enablement for every stage of your development. 

*The following EGI sessions will run throughout the year, with quarterly updates to the schedule. 
Each session is limited to 30 individual customer participants.  
**Registration links below direct to SAP Learning content for SAP Enterprise Support, which requires a one-time registration with a valid S-user.

Configuration, Administration, Security and Monitoring topics for SAP HANA Cloud

In this live EGI session we will guide you through the SAP HANA Cloud capabilities as DBaaS, demonstrate the key features available in the latest quarterly release of the tool and show the benefits of using the administration tools for DB Monitoring, security and configuration. For details, please read the abstract here.

Agenda: 

  • Introduction to SAP HANA Cloud
  • Overview of SAP BTP
  • Introduction and usage of Administration tools
    • SAP HANA Cloud Central
    • SAP HANA Cockpit
    • SAP HANA Database Explorer
  • Managing, Monitoring and Alert Notification in SAP HANA Cloud
  • Workload Management:
    • Managing Tables
    • HANA NSE
  • SAP HANA HDI Overview
  • Introduction and usage of Cloud Foundry CLI 
  • Security Administration and User management

Register Here* 

 

SAP HANA Cloud Modeling

In this live EGI session, we will guide you through the process of modeling in SAP HANA Cloud. After the completion of the session, you will have the knowledge of calculation view usage and design in SAP HANA Cloud utilizing the SAP Business Application studio. Also, we showcase the core capabilities of SAP HANA Cloud for calculation view graphical modelling and provide help on about SQL Script procedures and implementing functions using the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP). For details, please read the abstract here.

Agenda: 

  • Introduction to SAP BTP and SAP HANA Cloud
  • Usage of SAP Business Application Studio and Administration tools
  • Deploy SAP Business Application Studio
  • Introduction and modeling Calculation views
  •  Modeling with SAP Business Application Studio and using Database Explorer
  • Modeling features
  • Generating restricted and calculated columns
  • SQL Script usage
  • Performance Analysis, Debug mode & SQL Analyser
  • Overview of Best Practices

 Register Here* 

 

SAP HANA Cloud Multiple Model Programming including Vector Engine and Knowledge Graph

In this live EGI session, we empower customers with the knowledge of SAP HANA Cloud multiple model programming using the latest concepts including vector engine, knowledge graph engine and retrieval augmented generation. After the completion of the workshop, customers will have the knowledge of multiple model engines and how to incorporate artificial intelligence and retrieval augmented generation capabilities.

Agenda: 

  • Introduction to SAP BTP and SAP HANA Cloud
  • Understand SAP HANA Cloud architecture and deployment options
  • Introduction to multiple model programming
  • Data Tiering considerations for multiple model programming
  • Overview of SAP Business Application Studio
  • Spatial data concepts in SAP HANA Cloud
  • Understanding spatial features and functions
  • Spatial data modeling and processing
  • Graph processing in SAP HANA Cloud
  • JSON document store in SAP HANA Cloud
  • Automated machine learning (AutoML) in SAP HANA Cloud
  • SAP HANA Cloud vector database
  • Capabilities and benefits of multiple model processing with vectors
  • SAP HANA Cloud knowledge graph engine
  • Knowledge graph and Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG)
  • Real-world applications for Retrieval Augmented Generation

Register Here* 

 

Pro-code cloud application programming development through SAP Build and AI (Joule)

In this live EGI session we will empower you with the knowledge of cloud application programming development in SAP HANA Cloud. After the completion of the session, you will have the knowledge of CAP CDS development and design in SAP HANA Cloud and understand how to build and deploying cloud application projects from scratch and with reduced costs utilizing pro-code development using SAP Build Code with Joule. For details, please read the abstract here.

Agenda:

  • Introduction to SAP BTP and SAP HANA Cloud
  • Introduction to SAP Build Code and Prompt Engineering
  • CAP Architecture and SAP Business Application Studio features
  • Build and deploy a CAP project using SAP Business Application Studio
  • Modeling in SAP HANA Cloud using CAP CDS
  • Modeling in SAP HANA Cloud Optimization
  • Building Application using SAP Build Code and Joule
  • Modeling in SAP HANA Cloud Management and Administration

Register Here* 

Learning Outcomes from the EGI Series:

  • Configuration, Administration, Security and Monitoring topics for SAP HANA Cloud:
    Master SAP HANA Cloud configuration, performance monitoring and secure user management using standard administration tools.
  • SAP HANA Cloud Modeling:
    Develop Calculation views and SQL Script procedures within SAP Business Application Studio.
  • SAP HANA Cloud Multiple Model Programming including Vector Engine and Knowledge Graph:
    Implement advanced Vector Engine, Knowledge Graph, and Spatial Data capabilities to power Generative AI and Retrieval Augmented Generation scenarios
  • Pro-code cloud application programming development through SAP Build and AI (Joule):
    Build and deploy end-to-end CAP applications efficiently using SAP Build Code and Joule


Who Should Attend? 

This workshop series is ideal for developers, modelers, business analysts, system administrators, database administrators and SAP customers looking to transform their business processes. Whether you're just getting started or want to go deeper into SAP's modern toolset, there’s a session tailored for your needs. 

 

GET MORE CONTENT LIKE THIS!

 SAP Enterprise Support Value Maps — your go-to team for Foundational Success Plan support and empowerment — have a dedicated SAP Community Group.

As part of your SAP cloud subscription, you get access to:

  • Expert-led resources
  • Tailored recommendations
  • Advisory services
  • …all designed to help you accelerate innovation and success.


👉 Visit our Community Group
Sign in with your S-user ID or Universal ID, then hit “Subscribe” to stay updated.

Looking to dive deeper into data-driven innovation?
Within the group, explore the Data & Analytics Value Map to connect with your Foundational Support team. Simply click “Join Group”, and once inside, you can even request a call with a topic expert to get personalized guidance and enablement support.

 

 

 

 

 

Quicklinks

  • SAP Business Data Cloud : Expert-Guided Implementation series
  • Ensuring a Smooth SAP Datasphere Deployment: The Value of Continuous Quality Checks
  • SAP Enterprise Support Value Maps - Community Group
  • SAP Enterprise Support - Guided Learning
